nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still freshwater habitats including ponds, ditches, marshes, the margins of lakes, and well-vegetated streams. Tolerant of low-oxygen water thanks to a haemoglobin-based blood pigment unusual among gastropods, which lets them survive in warm, weedy environments other snails struggle in.

decorPreferences: live plants for grazing and biofilm
tightly fitting lid (occasional climbers above the waterline)

diet.notes: Generally considered an opportunistic grazer. Readily consumes soft green and brown algae, biofilm, decaying plant matter, leftover fish food, sinking pellets, algae wafers, and blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, cucumber, lettuce). A calcium source (cuttlebone, crushed coral, calcium-rich foods) supports healthy shell development; in soft water shell erosion has been reported. Live plants are generally left alone when other food is plentiful, though underfed populations have been observed nibbling soft-leaved species (Cabomba, hornwort) — hard-leaved plants (Anubias, Java fern, Bucephalandra) are essentially untouched.

goodWith: small peaceful community fish (tetras, rasboras, livebearers)
avoidWith: snail-eating loaches (clown loach, yoyo loach, zebra loach)

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Dat doris. 3614x2711. Vibrant red/pink trade morph on pale substrate, whole animal sharp and well-lit, flat planispiral 'ram's horn' shell and red foot clearly shown — the classic aquarium look.
Photographer: Dat doris. 3970x2978. Amber/orange morph climbing a plant, face-on view showing the diagnostic tight planispiral spiral and pink-red body; sharp and colourful.

Photographer: Dat doris. 5152x3864. Group of two-three snails showing pink-leucistic and orange colour forms together; crisp detail of the coiled shell and red body, illustrates colour variation and social grazing.

summary: The Ramshorn Snail is a small, flat-shelled freshwater snail reaching about 1.5 to 2.5 cm across, widely traded in red, pink, blue, and leopard color forms. It reproduces easily in home aquaria without special effort and is a common hitchhiker on live plants.
careNotes: Ramshorn snails are widely regarded as one of the most beginner-friendly freshwater invertebrates. This entry covers the cluster of species sold interchangeably as 'ramshorn snail' in the aquarium trade: Planorbella duryi (the most common trade animal, around 1.5–2 cm), Planorbella trivolvis (known in the trade as the Great Ramshorn, native to North America, slightly larger at around 2–3 cm), and Planorbarius corneus (the European Great Ramshorn, the largest planorbid commonly traded at around 2.5–3.5 cm). The older genus name Helisoma — still seen on some stock lists — refers to species now reclassified into Planorbella. Color morphs — Pink, Red (a leucistic phase in which the haemoglobin-rich body pigment shows through the shell), Blue, and Leopard (mottled) — all share identical care requirements; the body color is genetic and breeds true within color lines. As with all freshwater invertebrates, ramshorn snails are highly copper-sensitive; copper-based fish medications and any plant fertilizer with elevated copper should be avoided in tanks where they are kept. They are simultaneous hermaphrodites, meaning a single snail is capable of self-fertilizing and founding a colony on its own, so populations can grow quickly in overfed tanks. Reducing the amount of fish food going into the aquarium is generally the most reliable way to slow population growth. One important distinction: Marisa cornuarietis, sometimes sold as the Giant Ramshorn or Colombian Ramshorn, shares a common name but is biologically quite different. It reaches 5–7.5 cm, belongs to a separate family (Ampullariidae rather than Planorbidae), and is a voracious consumer of live plants that will systematically work through a planted aquarium regardless of how well it is fed. It represents a separate husbandry case from the smaller Planorbella species despite the overlapping trade name.
breedingNotes: Generally considered effortless — most keepers find ramshorn snails breed without intervention and rather wish for the opposite. They are simultaneous hermaphrodites: a single snail can self-fertilize, and any two adults can reproduce. Gelatinous egg clutches of 10–20+ eggs are deposited on plant leaves, glass, and hardscape, and typically hatch in 2–3 weeks at room temperature. Population growth tracks food availability closely; reducing fish-food input is the standard population-control lever. Assassin snails (Clea helena) and snail-eating loaches will also crash a ramshorn population.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Aquarium Co-Op's primary published treatment of ramshorn snails is within their pest-snail control article; they do not host a dedicated ramshorn care guide. Used as the slice primary on the strength of brand consistency.

additional.name: The Shrimp Farm
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 on access and could not be cross-checked. Listed for traceability; swapped to Aquarium Source and Aquarium Breeder as substitute secondaries.

additional.name: Aquarium Source
additional.notes: Substitute secondary. Confirmed family Planorbidae, ~2.5 cm adult size, 21–26°C, pH 7.0–7.5, kH 5–15, lifespan 1–3 years, hermaphroditic reproduction, 5 gal minimum tank.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder
additional.notes: Substitute secondary. Confirmed Planorbella duryi / Planorbarius corneus species coverage, adult size 0.5–2.5 cm, temperature 21–25°C, pH 7–8, GH 7–15, KH 5–15, lifespan 1–3 years, peaceful temperament, and live-plant nibbling only when underfed.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder (Marisa cornuarietis)
additional.notes: Used solely to inform the Marisa cornuarietis (Giant / Colombian Ramshorn) split note in careNotes — larger size (5–7.5 cm), Ampullariidae family, voracious live-plant consumption, distinct husbandry case.
